name: Elizabeth Gurley Flynn
description: Labor organizer, feminist, and Communist Party leader known as 'The Rebel Girl' (the title of Joe Hill's 1915 song, dedicated to her). Born in Concord, New Hampshire in 1890 to Irish immigrant parents, she gave her first public socialist speech at age 15 and became a full-time IWW organizer at 17. She participated in the 1909 Spokane free-speech fight, the 1912 Lawrence textile strike, the 1913 Paterson silk strike, the 1916 Mesabi Range iron miners' strike, and defense work for Joe Hill, Tom Mooney, Sacco and Vanzetti, and the IWW Espionage Act prisoners. A founding member of the American Civil Liberties Union in 1920, she was expelled from its board in 1940 for her Communist Party membership. She joined the CPUSA in 1936, became a member of its National Committee, and was elected National Chairman in 1961. In 1951 she was indicted under the Smith Act in the 'second-string' Foley Square prosecution of CPUSA leaders; convicted in 1953, she entered the Federal Reformatory for Women at Alderson, West Virginia in January 1955. She served approximately 28 months and was released in May 1957, later writing her prison memoir 'The Alderson Story: My Life as a Political Prisoner.' She died on a visit to Moscow on September 5, 1964, and received a state funeral in Red Square.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to teach and advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government by force and violence (Smith Act, 18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Morrie R. Preston
description: Morrie R. Preston was a young IWW-aligned restaurant cook in Goldfield, Nevada who, during the bitter 1907 Goldfield Consolidated Mines strike, shot and killed restaurant owner John Silva in what he maintained was self-defense. Mine operators and the Nevada Mine Owners' Association seized on the killing to frame Preston as a Western Federation of Miners assassin, securing a 25-year murder conviction widely viewed by labor historians as a frame-up. Preston was paroled in 1914 after a sustained labor defense campaign and posthumously pardoned by the Nevada Board of Pardons in 1987.

cases.charges: Murder (shooting of restaurant owner John Silva during the Goldfield miners' strike)
cases.convicted: Yes — second-degree murder; widely regarded as a frame-up

name: Joseph W. Smith
description: Joseph W. Smith was the co-defendant of Morrie R. Preston in the 1907 Goldfield, Nevada frame-up that grew out of the Goldfield Consolidated Mines strike led by the Western Federation of Miners and the IWW. Smith was convicted of manslaughter for his alleged role in the killing of restaurant owner John Silva and sentenced to 10 years at Nevada State Prison. He was paroled in 1911 after a national labor-defense campaign exposed the prosecution as a mine-owner reprisal against striking workers.

cases.charges: Manslaughter (co-defendant in the Goldfield Silva killing)

name: Joseph Ettor
description: Joseph Ettor was an Italian-American IWW organizer who, with Arturo Giovannitti, led the 1912 Bread and Roses textile strike of 23,000 workers in Lawrence, Massachusetts. After striker Anna LoPizzo was shot dead by police on January 29, 1912, Ettor and Giovannitti — miles away addressing a strike meeting — were arrested as accessories to murder in an explicit attempt to behead the strike. After nearly a year in jail awaiting trial in Salem, they were acquitted in November 1912 following one of the most celebrated labor-defense campaigns of the early 20th century.

cases.charges: Accessory to murder (Anna LoPizzo, Lawrence textile strike)

name: Arturo Giovannitti
description: Arturo Giovannitti was an Italian-American radical poet, editor of Il Proletario, and IWW organizer who co-led the 1912 Lawrence Bread and Roses textile strike with Joseph Ettor. Arrested on January 30, 1912 as an accessory to the murder of striker Anna LoPizzo despite being miles from the scene, he spent nearly a year in the Essex County Jail in Salem, where he wrote his most famous poem, 'The Walker.' He and Ettor were acquitted in November 1912 in one of the great labor-defense victories of the IWW era.

cases.charges: Accessory to murder (Anna LoPizzo, Lawrence textile strike)

name: Joseph Caruso
description: Joseph Caruso was an Italian immigrant textile worker in Lawrence, Massachusetts and a rank-and-file striker during the 1912 Bread and Roses strike. He was the third defendant — alongside IWW organizers Joseph Ettor and Arturo Giovannitti — charged as an accessory to the police killing of striker Anna LoPizzo in a transparent attempt to crush the strike. Caruso was acquitted along with Ettor and Giovannitti by an Essex County jury in November 1912.

cases.charges: Accessory to murder (Anna LoPizzo, Lawrence textile strike)

name: Patrick Quinlan
description: Patrick Quinlan was an Irish-American IWW orator and Socialist Party organizer who spoke alongside Elizabeth Gurley Flynn, Carlo Tresca, and Big Bill Haywood at the 1913 Paterson, New Jersey silk strike of 25,000 workers. He was the first of the strike's leaders convicted under New Jersey's archaic 'unlawful assemblage' and incitement statutes, drawing 2 to 7 years at the New Jersey State Prison in Trenton. After exhausting appeals he was paroled in 1916 following a sustained national defense campaign.

cases.charges: Incitement / unlawful assemblage (Paterson silk strike speech)

name: Alexander Scott
description: Alexander Scott was the editor of the Passaic Weekly Issue, a socialist newspaper that covered the 1913 Paterson silk strike sympathetically. He was prosecuted under New Jersey's 1902 Criminal Anarchy Law — passed after the McKinley assassination — for editorials condemning Paterson police violence against strikers. Sentenced to 1 to 15 years at the New Jersey State Prison in Trenton, his case became a landmark First Amendment fight for the early labor and free-speech movements.

cases.charges: Violation of the New Jersey Criminal Anarchy Law (1902)

name: Frederick Sumner Boyd
description: Frederick Sumner Boyd was a British-born IWW soapboxer and former Socialist Party speaker who traveled to Paterson during the 1913 silk strike. He was convicted under New Jersey's Criminal Anarchy Law for a speech in which he allegedly advocated industrial sabotage — specifically the use of dyes to ruin scab-produced silk. Sentenced to 1 to 7 years at the New Jersey State Prison in Trenton, his conviction was the most notorious 'sabotage' prosecution of the pre-war IWW.

cases.charges: Violation of the New Jersey Criminal Anarchy Law (advocacy of sabotage during the Paterson silk strike)

name: Vincent Buccafori
description: Vincent Buccafori was an Italian immigrant shoe-worker in Brooklyn, New York who in 1911 killed his foreman in what he and his defenders described as self-defense after years of workplace abuse. Initially convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to death, his sentence was commuted to 15 years and later reduced to 10 years at Sing Sing following a major Italian-American labor-defense campaign by the IWW, the Industrial Workers Defense Conference, and Elizabeth Gurley Flynn. He became a touchstone case for working-class self-defense in the pre-WWI labor press.

cases.charges: Murder (workplace killing of his foreman)
cases.convicted: Yes — initially first-degree murder, commuted to 15 years and reduced to 10 years

name: Frank Tannenbaum
description: Frank Tannenbaum was a 21-year-old Austrian-born IWW unemployed-workers' organizer in New York City during the brutal winter of 1913-14. In March 1914 he led a band of homeless men into St. Alphonsus Catholic Church on West Broadway demanding food and shelter, in the most famous of his 'army of the unemployed' church occupations. Convicted of unlawful assembly, he served one year on Blackwell's Island and went on to become a distinguished Columbia historian of Latin America and U.S. labor.

cases.charges: Unlawful assembly (St. Alphonsus Church occupation)

name: Charles Cline
description: Charles Cline was an IWW organizer who in September 1913 was part of a group of radical labor activists arrested at Carrizo Springs, Texas while attempting to cross into Mexico to join the Magonista revolutionary movement. After a gunfight with a Texas posse left a deputy dead, Cline was convicted of murder and sentenced to 25 years to life at the Texas State Penitentiary in Huntsville. He was pardoned in 1926 by Texas Governor Miriam 'Ma' Ferguson after a long defense campaign by the IWW, the Mexican Liberal Party, and the General Defense Committee.

cases.charges: Murder (Carrizo Springs border gunfight)
cases.convicted: Yes — murder, pardoned 1926 by Gov. Miriam A. Ferguson

name: Matthew Schmidt
description: Matthew Schmidt was an anarchist and union ironworker connected to the McNamara brothers' bombing of the Los Angeles Times building on October 1, 1910, which killed 21 workers and shattered the Open Shop war between organized labor and the National Erectors' Association. After years as a fugitive, Schmidt was captured in 1915, convicted of murder in connection with the bombing, and sentenced to life imprisonment at San Quentin and later Folsom.
affiliation: International Association of Bridge and Structural Iron Workers

cases.charges: Murder (Los Angeles Times building bombing of October 1, 1910)

name: David Caplan
description: David Caplan was an anarchist grocery-store keeper on Bainbridge Island, Washington who, along with Matthew Schmidt, was indicted in connection with the October 1, 1910 bombing of the Los Angeles Times building during the Open Shop war led by Times publisher Harrison Gray Otis. After two hung juries, Caplan was convicted of second-degree murder in 1916 and sentenced to 10 years at San Quentin. His case was a major labor-defense cause supported by the AFL Structural Ironworkers and the Industrial Workers of the World.

cases.charges: Murder (Los Angeles Times building bombing of October 1, 1910)

name: Tom Mooney
description: Thomas Joseph Mooney was a San Francisco Irish-American socialist and militant labor organizer in the iron molders' and streetcar workers' unions. He was framed for the July 22, 1916 Preparedness Day Parade bombing in San Francisco that killed 10 people, convicted on perjured testimony, and sentenced to death — a sentence later commuted to life imprisonment at San Quentin and Folsom by Governor William Stephens under enormous international pressure. After 22 years of imprisonment, he was unconditionally pardoned on January 7, 1939 by California Governor Culbert Olson in one of the most famous labor frame-up reversals in U.S. history.

cases.charges: Murder (San Francisco Preparedness Day Parade bombing of July 22, 1916)
cases.convicted: Yes — death sentence, commuted to life 1918, fully pardoned January 7, 1939 by Gov. Culbert Olson
cases.sentence: Death, commuted to life, then unconditional pardon after 22 years

name: Warren K. Billings
description: Warren K. Billings was a young shoe-worker and union militant who, like Tom Mooney, was framed for the July 22, 1916 Preparedness Day Parade bombing in San Francisco. Convicted in September 1916 on the perjured testimony of witness Frank Oxman, he was sentenced to life imprisonment at Folsom State Prison, where he served 23 years. He was paroled in 1939 and finally received a full pardon from California Governor Earl Warren in 1961.

cases.charges: Murder (San Francisco Preparedness Day Parade bombing of July 22, 1916)
cases.convicted: Yes — life imprisonment; paroled 1939, full pardon by Gov. Earl Warren in 1961

name: Israel Weinberg
description: Israel Weinberg was a San Francisco jitney driver and president of the Jitney Bus Operators' Union, indicted along with Tom Mooney, Warren Billings, Rena Mooney, and Edward Nolan for the July 22, 1916 Preparedness Day Parade bombing. The prosecution alleged that he had driven the bombers to the scene in his jitney. He was acquitted at trial in 1917 after the prosecution's case collapsed.

cases.charges: Murder (San Francisco Preparedness Day Parade bombing of July 22, 1916)

name: Edward D. Nolan
description: Edward D. Nolan was a machinist and officer of the International Association of Machinists in San Francisco, indicted along with Tom Mooney, Warren Billings, Israel Weinberg, and Rena Mooney for the July 22, 1916 Preparedness Day Parade bombing. The case against him collapsed and he was never brought to trial; the charges were dismissed in 1918 after Mooney's and Billings's convictions had already been secured.

cases.charges: Murder (San Francisco Preparedness Day Parade bombing of July 22, 1916)

name: Rena Mooney
description: Rena Mooney was a San Francisco music teacher, socialist activist, and the wife of Tom Mooney. She was indicted along with her husband, Warren Billings, Israel Weinberg, and Edward Nolan for the July 22, 1916 Preparedness Day Parade bombing, after photographs purported to place her at the scene. She was acquitted by jury in July 1917 and went on to lead the international defense campaign that ultimately freed her husband in 1939.

cases.charges: Murder (San Francisco Preparedness Day Parade bombing of July 22, 1916)

name: John R. Lawson
description: John R. Lawson was a United Mine Workers of America organizer and a leader of the 1913-14 Colorado Coalfield War, which culminated in the Ludlow Massacre of April 20, 1914 in which Colorado National Guard troops killed roughly two dozen striking miners and their families. In 1915 Lawson was singled out for prosecution and convicted of the murder of guard John Mimms during the strike, drawing a life sentence at the Colorado State Penitentiary in Cañon City. The Colorado Supreme Court reversed the conviction in April 1918, and the charges were ultimately dropped.

cases.charges: Murder of guard John Mimms (Colorado Coalfield War / Ludlow strike)
cases.convicted: Yes — life sentence, reversed by Colorado Supreme Court April 1918

name: Thomas H. Tracy
description: Thomas H. Tracy was an IWW shingle weaver and free-speech fighter selected as the test defendant in the prosecutions arising from the November 5, 1916 Everett Massacre, in which Snohomish County sheriff's deputies fired on the IWW steamer Verona at the Everett, Washington dock, killing at least five Wobblies and two deputies. He and 73 IWW members were charged with murdering deputy Jefferson Beard. Tracy was acquitted by a King County jury in May 1917 and the charges against the other 73 defendants were dropped.

cases.charges: Murder (Everett Massacre — death of deputy Jefferson Beard)
cases.convicted: No — acquitted May 5, 1917 by King County jury

name: Britt Smith
description: Britt Smith was secretary of the Centralia, Washington IWW local and one of the seven Wobblies convicted in the prosecutions arising from the Centralia Tragedy of November 11, 1919, when American Legionnaires marching in an Armistice Day parade attacked the IWW union hall and four Legionnaires were killed in the ensuing gunfight. Smith was convicted of second-degree murder and sentenced to 25 to 40 years at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla. He served until paroled in 1933 after a sustained General Defense Committee campaign.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)

name: O.C. Bland
description: O.C. Bland was a member of the Centralia, Washington IWW local and one of the seven Wobblies convicted following the Centralia Tragedy of November 11, 1919. Convicted of second-degree murder, he was sentenced to 25 to 40 years at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla and was paroled in the early 1930s following the long Centralia defense campaign.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)

name: Bert Bland
description: Bert Bland was a logger and IWW member at the Centralia, Washington local, and the brother of O.C. Bland. He was among the seven Wobblies convicted of second-degree murder following the November 11, 1919 Centralia Tragedy. Bland was sentenced to 25 to 40 years at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla and paroled in the early 1930s.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)

name: James McInerney
description: James McInerney was an IWW member at the Centralia, Washington local and one of the seven Wobblies convicted of second-degree murder for the November 11, 1919 Centralia Tragedy. He served at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla under a 25-to-40-year sentence, and died of tuberculosis behind bars in 1930 before the rest of the Centralia defendants were paroled.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)
cases.convicted: Yes — second-degree murder; died in prison of tuberculosis

name: Ray Becker
description: Ray Becker was an IWW member at the Centralia, Washington local and one of the seven Wobblies convicted of second-degree murder for the November 11, 1919 Centralia Tragedy. He refused all offers of parole on principle, demanding a full pardon and exoneration, and consequently served longer than any other Centralia defendant — until finally released by sentence commutation in 1939 after nearly 20 years.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)
cases.convicted: Yes — second-degree murder; refused parole, released by commutation in 1939

name: Eugene Barnett
description: Eugene Barnett was a coal miner and IWW member at the Centralia, Washington local and one of the seven Wobblies convicted of second-degree murder for the November 11, 1919 Centralia Tragedy. Sentenced to 25 to 40 years at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla, he became one of the most articulate prison correspondents of the Centralia defense campaign and was paroled in 1931.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)

name: John Lamb
description: John Lamb was an IWW logger at the Centralia, Washington local and one of the seven Wobblies convicted of second-degree murder for the November 11, 1919 Centralia Tragedy. He served at the Washington State Penitentiary in Walla Walla under a 25-to-40-year sentence and was paroled in the early 1930s.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder (Centralia Armistice Day shootout, November 11, 1919)

name: Wesley Everest
description: Wesley Everest was an IWW logger, decorated World War I army veteran, and member of the Centralia, Washington Wobbly local. During the Centralia Tragedy of November 11, 1919, after Legionnaires raided the IWW hall, he was beaten, dragged from the city jail that night, mutilated, and lynched from the Chehalis River bridge by a mob — making him one of the most notorious labor-movement lynchings in U.S. history. No one was ever prosecuted for his murder, which the local coroner ruled a suicide.

cases.charges: Held in connection with the Centralia Armistice Day shootout; killed by lynch mob before any trial
cases.convicted: No — lynched by mob the night of his arrest

name: Elizabeth Ford
description: Elizabeth Ford was a Socialist Labor Party editor in Faribault, Minnesota who was convicted under the Espionage Act of 1917 for anti-war agitation during World War I. She was sentenced to one year in prison and a $500 fine — one of a small group of women editors swept up by federal wartime prosecutions of socialist publications.

cases.charges: Violation of the Espionage Act of 1917

name: Elizabeth Baer
description: Dr. Elizabeth Baer was the recording secretary of the Philadelphia Socialist Party who, with Charles Schenck, was prosecuted under the Espionage Act of 1917 for mailing 15,000 leaflets urging draftees to resist conscription as 'involuntary servitude' prohibited by the Thirteenth Amendment. Convicted in 1918, she was sentenced to 90 days in jail. The companion case Schenck v. United States (1919) produced Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes's famous 'clear and present danger' test.

cases.charges: Violation of the Espionage Act of 1917 (anti-conscription leaflet — Schenck v. United States companion case)

name: Eugene Dennis
description: Eugene Dennis (born Francis Xavier Waldron Jr.) was General Secretary of the Communist Party USA and the lead defendant in the 1949 Foley Square trial, in which eleven CPUSA leaders were prosecuted under the Smith Act of 1940. Convicted in October 1949, he was sentenced to five years in federal prison and served at the U.S. Penitentiary in Atlanta. He was released in 1955 and led the CPUSA through the McCarthyite period until his death in 1961.

cases.charges: Conspiracy under the Smith Act of 1940 (1949 Foley Square trial)

name: Henry M. Winston
description: National Organization Secretary of the Communist Party USA. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years for conspiracy to advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government; jumped bail in 1951 and served additional time after surrendering in 1956, losing his sight to an untreated brain tumor in federal prison.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385) — conspiracy to teach and advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government by force and violence
cases.sentence: 5 years plus additional time for bail-jumping

name: William Z. Foster
description: National Chairman of the Communist Party USA and former leader of the 1919 steel strike. Indicted in July 1948 in the Foley Square Smith Act case but severed from the 1949 trial due to a serious heart condition; never tried and never imprisoned.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385) — conspiracy to teach and advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government by force and violence
cases.convicted: No — severed from trial due to ill health; never tried

name: John B. Williamson
description: Labor Secretary of the Communist Party USA and a Scottish-born CP organizer. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years; after serving his term he was deported to the United Kingdom in 1955.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Jacob Stachel
description: Education Secretary of the Communist Party USA and a longtime trade union organizer. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years in federal prison for conspiracy to advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Robert G. Thompson
description: New York State Chairman of the Communist Party USA and a decorated World War II combat veteran (Distinguished Service Cross, New Guinea). Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 3 years; jumped bail in 1951, was captured in the Sierra Nevada in 1953, and served additional time for bail-jumping, including a period at Alcatraz.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385) plus bail-jumping

name: Benjamin J. Davis Jr.
description: New York City Councilman from Harlem (1943–1949) and a leading Black Communist. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years, which he served largely at the federal penitentiary in Terre Haute, Indiana; expelled from the City Council upon his conviction.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: John Gates
description: Editor of the Daily Worker and a Spanish Civil War veteran of the Abraham Lincoln Brigade. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years; later broke publicly with the CPUSA in 1958 after the Khrushchev revelations and the Soviet invasion of Hungary.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Irving Potash
description: Leader of the Furriers Joint Council in New York and a longtime Communist trade unionist. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years; deported to Poland after his release as a foreign-born Communist, later returning illegally and serving additional time.
affiliation: International Fur and Leather Workers Union

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Gilbert Green
description: Illinois State Chairman of the Communist Party USA and a former national leader of the Young Communist League. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years; jumped bail in 1951, surrendered in 1956, and served additional time for bail-jumping at Leavenworth.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385) plus bail-jumping

name: Carl Winter
description: Michigan State Chairman of the Communist Party USA. Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years for conspiracy to advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government; later served as editor of the Daily World after his release.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Gus Hall
description: Ohio State Chairman of the Communist Party USA at the time of his 1948 indictment and later National General Secretary of the CPUSA (1959–2000). Convicted in the 1949 Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced to 5 years; jumped bail in 1951, fled to Mexico, was captured and served additional time for bail-jumping at Leavenworth.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385) plus bail-jumping

name: Pettis Perry
description: Alabama-born Black sharecropper turned Communist Party USA organizer and chair of the Party's National Negro Commission. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years; after his release he emigrated to the Soviet Union, where he died.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Claudia Jones
description: Trinidadian-born Black feminist Communist, journalist, and theorist of the 'triple oppression' of Black women. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to a year and a day at the Federal Reformatory for Women in Alderson, West Virginia; deported to the United Kingdom in 1955, where she founded the Notting Hill Carnival.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Alexander Bittelman
description: Founding member of the Communist Party USA, longtime theoretician, and editor of Political Affairs. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years; faced deportation proceedings after his release as a foreign-born Communist.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: V. J. Jerome
description: Cultural editor of the Communist Party USA and chair of its Cultural Commission, overseeing the Party's literary and arts work. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Alexander Trachtenberg
description: Ukrainian-born founder and head of International Publishers, the principal Communist publishing house in the United States. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years; his conviction was overturned on retrial after the Supreme Court's 1957 Yates decision.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Betty Gannett
description: Polish-born Education Director of the Communist Party USA and longtime staff member of the Party's national education department. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 2 years at the Federal Reformatory for Women in Alderson.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Arnold Johnson
description: Eastern Educational Director and later Legislative Director of the Communist Party USA. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Jacob Mindel
description: Veteran instructor at the Communist Party USA's Jefferson School of Social Science in New York and a leading Party educator. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953; given a reduced 1-year-and-1-day term in light of his advanced age and ill health.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Louis Weinstock
description: Hungarian-born official of the United Furniture Workers and former general secretary of the Painters District Council in New York. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: William W. Weinstone
description: Veteran Communist Party USA leader and Michigan State Organizer, formerly head of the Party's Detroit district during the auto-organizing drives of the 1930s. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Albert Lannon
description: Maritime organizer for the Communist Party USA and a longtime waterfront union leader. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Isidore Begun
description: Bronx Communist Party USA leader and longtime educator and organizer in New York's public schools and teachers' union. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 2 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: George Blake Charney
description: Manhattan organizer for the Communist Party USA and author of the political memoir 'A Long Journey.' Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years; left the CPUSA in 1958 in the wake of the Khrushchev revelations.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Fred Fine
description: Midwestern Communist Party USA leader and one of the four Smith Act defendants who went underground in 1951 rather than surrender to begin serving their sentences. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years; surrendered in 1956 and served additional time for bail-jumping.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385) plus bail-jumping
cases.sentence: 3 years (Smith Act) plus additional time for bail-jumping

name: Simon W. Gerson
description: Political action director of the Communist Party USA in New York and the Party's perennial candidate for public office. Convicted in the 1952 second-string Foley Square Smith Act trial and sentenced in January 1953 to 3 years.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)

name: Marion Bachrach
description: Communist Party USA journalist and longtime aide to Congressman John Bernard, sister of CP leader John Abt. Indicted in the 1951 second-string Foley Square Smith Act case but severed from the 1952 trial after she was diagnosed with terminal cancer; never convicted.

cases.charges: Smith Act (18 U.S.C. § 2385)
cases.convicted: No — severed from trial due to terminal illness; never tried
